author: @moking8095
text: Hi there Rob, I have enjoyed the wide range of subjects that you seem to command. However, your explanations of some of the effects of wind generators are a little shaky and the amount of power generated is only a small fraction of your claim of a 1000watt wind generator. Having said that I do think that the size and quantity of magnets you employ would easily be capable of said watts but so far you seem to drift away from the true path to 1KW of power. The use of MW turntable motors is a very poor direction for the desired 1KW. Secondly the use of MW transformers is a far better direction to take although at 1KW output the cogging effect would be a great barrier to overcome. The addition of rectification and 1000UF smoothing did produce a somewhat unusual anti-cogging effect but would change the total output to| DC power instead of the more controllable AC power. I am not saying that you are wrong but that you need to stay focused on the main problem namely producing at the very least a 1/2 KW of AC power which can be used to off-set the main heating bills of the average home electricity consumer. Your videos are great and I hope I have not put any spokes in your wheel. My intention is to redirect your efforts into a more productive direction. Regards MoK

Couldn't you go another route to save on copper? One of the major issues with low power distributed generators is that if they ever became realistic, at least in the style you made, it would be copper intensive. The cores allow more power to be utilized from less copper.
Instead of going scoreless and using more wire, couldn't you have instead used leverage to get smaller force converted to higher force? Say, rig up a belt and various pulley sizes, where the fan portion spins a couple times to spin the generator wheel. You would still get low power, but the ratio of spins would give enough force to push against the coil with a core in it and it would generate more power per ounce of copper.
I don't know the viabilities of this, just asking your thoughts on feasibility and possibly copper savings.

author: @Talking-Universe-2025
text:
I see a deficiency in the design that will cause a potential large loss of electromagnetic  power creation. Passing a magnet over a coil whereby the majority or entire coil is doped by the field of a single magnet pole, t won't create any power.  Yes there will be power as each East/West side of the coil is seeing opposing magnetic fields on either side by any 2 opposing N, S magnet pairs  but a magnet overhead will create none which is lost potential. This is why motors conventionally have iron cores to direct the magnetic field 90° into a more 'push/pull' field down through the coil which gives the best outcome of the right hand rule for current flow from conductor in moving magnetic field. As a dodgy simple test, why not throw a pile of steel washers in through coil hole (then secured by a taper head screw for eg) and compare 🙏. I understand why you chose to not have cores due to magneto resistance but that's disposing of efficiency for the sake of startup torque. A solution for that is to selectively switch in circuit sections to the load once enough power is generated at a small section of coils circuit level which can be easily automated with a transistor switch. 
Its the actual load that will play the biggest part in impeding start torque.
Washers in cores, transistor switch to connect 3/4 of circuit to load once 1/4 of circuit has seen enough power from low resistance intital startup.

author: @alansmith2292
text:
Really good video(s) love your enthusiasm. Where I live in the foothills of the pennines the average wind speed is 6.6m/s. I think with a residential turbine you'll do well if you get 70w on average with a good small HAWT or VAWT from 6.6. You might be able to run a 40w heat lamp in a chicken coop after taking into account inverter losses etc. Yes on really windy days you could peak at 400-500w because you'll have to furl or pitch at the rated wind speed (usually 10-12m/s) to avoid self-destructing the turbine. You'll never see the the true (risky) potential from wind speed x 2 = power x 8.

I don't think the manufacturers are to blame (except some of the Chinese, I saw one sub-2m blade diameter claiming 8KW) I think customers latch on to the rated and ignore advice to check local average and install as high as possible.)

To be fair Robert I watched this series hoping to see you hit 1KW but reality kicked in and you said the objective was to harvest energy from wind speeds. Not true, your objective was to generate 1KW from a turbine coating less than £100 (or was it $100?) Anyway, it doesn't matter, a reality check for everyone and I enjoyed watching.

author: @marcelthomassen3152
text:
First of all, kudos for showing us your ideas and knowledge, thank you so much! This vawt is interesting for MANY people as it is “simple”, low cost and can be made with several different materials by most people with a “left and right hand”.
You stated “low wind – low speed and low cost”. What if you make a dual vawt upon each other that turn counter wise of each other, one being the rotor (top bottom vawt) and the other one being the stator (bottom top vawt)? You would still have the “low cost and low speed” but you would increase the speed significantly. The wiring could be done with a slip ring on the (hollow) stator shaft. Probably this dual vawt would be MUCH smaller as the 80 cm unit you have now but with the same output. I am mentioning a smaller version as it will be easier to make a smaller dual version. 
Basically with a dual version you double the speed, it would be interesting to know the output with the unit you have now with a speed of X and a speed of X+50% and 2X.  That would give you an indication of the effect of higher speed and maybe a feeling how much smaller you could go.
A smaller version (like 40 – 45 cm) you could probably put on a pole as it will be much lighter. I mention 40 – 45 cm as I have that in my head but I would be surprised if I would be far off lol.
Anyway, food for thought for everyone who reads this and it is now in the public domain as far as it wasn’t already. Thanks again!

author: @dchall8
text: I'm going to criticize for the choice of a vertical mounted rotor.  The thrust bearing at the bottom (you did use a proper thrust bearing, right?), the thrust bearing needs to be lubricated periodically.  Do you have a way to do that?  Also, one big reason why you never see vertically mounted commercial rotors is that the thrust bearing quickly clogs with dirt and sand requiring a replacement of the bearing...assuming it doesn't fail catastrophically before you notice the grit wearing it out.  Mounting the rotor 300 feet in the air nearly alleviates the grit in the bearings problem.

author: @nerminhamidovic638
text: Not sure if this diode or-ring arrangement will work efficiently. Diodes aren't ideal. they leak. there are now also as many VI losses through the diodes. The coil that produces the highest voltage will try to drive the other coils at least in an open circuit. This is also probably the reason why you only got very little emf on the output. It would be way better to reconfigure this as a poly-phase stator, 3, 6 or 12 phases depending on the efficiency and input voltage requirements of the inverter that would be used to generate the 240V AC

author: @gravelydon7072
text: One of the ways to cut down on the cogging would be to install a second set of coils spaced 1/2 the distance of your first set on a second level with the magnets in the middle. That way, as one set of magnets is coming on to a coil, the second set of magnets would be already centered. Stack 4 sets and 2 sets of magnets and adjust the spacing to 1/4th per level  and cogging will disappear.

author: @claytonmccormick622
text: windmills are no well suited to making electricity  . and most current designs put everything at the top of a pole.but if you seperate the generator from from whatever you use to pump by adding storage you can do that , and get several advantages .windmills do high rpm constant duty very badly but if you add storage to this equation than you can set it up so the generator runs at high rpm constant duty .you can pump air into a pressure vessel you can pump water uphill you  can even lift a heavy rock with a set of pulleys when you do this you can put most of the moving parts on the ground where they are easy to get to and work on and because we are using energy storage you can use as many windmills as you like a good size for the generator part would be a gm 90 amp alternator is a good choice because you can  get a refurbished one for about 35 dollars and because it is gm it will be one of the most common parts in the world available but if your parts car still has an alternator you could use that for free. by doing this you can make several different windmills to store power to run the alternator.  pumping water uphill is a very cheap way to store power but any storage method will work . back in the 90's mother earth news did a multi month story about a physics class at the university of arizona were the clas built a vw with a hydraulic storage transmission  the first thing they figured out was the 57 h.p. was way to  big so they replaced it with a 16 h.p. and still up the top speed and lowered to 0 to 60 quite a bit sadly the professor was hired by the ford motor company.the next year so there were no more stories they only did a mileage test once in a competition sadly the bypass valuev was mounted in such a way that it ratelled shut disabling the fluid bypass circuit though they still got 90 miles to the gallon .the point being while in the short term  wind is unpredictable in the long run it is very predictable so if you can store the power when you can the storage will level out the available power and one of the cheapest ways to store power is to pump water uphill. sorry about the long wordage i just wanted to say enough so as to pass the idea along to others.

author: @totherarf
text:
I think with commercial generators they sell by promising a Peak Load ..... which they fail to produce!
When you look at the way they are built (stator armature etc) it is unrealistic to expect quoted performance of them in anything but peak conditions and vastly lower at normal conditions! A bit like wondering why your solar PV system in northern Scotland does not give as much power as those in California ;0)
It would be interesting to see what the output of say 10 of your design generators would produce against an equivalently priced turbine!
There may be room for improvement in your device by replacing the "lazy Susan"! The problem (IMO) is that a lazy ssusan is designed to allow an weight to be revolved not spun. Getting a circular trck you could run such a device round may be more trouble than it's worth though ;0)

author: @MichaelDobbins
text:
If you had turned the coils around with the rectifier and capacitor on the inner side, you would better protect the electronics from the weather, the DC bus would be shorter and it would look cleaner without a cover.  
I love this design.  I am playing with the vibration tube with an artificial muscle generator you built in another video. I am planning on making multiple vibration tubes and tie them together with a similar coil/rectifier/capacitor/DC bus you have here.  
Would a blocking diode give any advantage by separating the capacitors of different performing units. Otherwise, all the capacitors will tend to be at the same level all the time possibly absorbing some of the energy into underperforming units that could have been delivered for use. Might be a consideration in this VAWT as you may have different specs in the microwave turntable coils from different manufacturers. Maybe the cost of the blocking diode would be more than you save with the separation.  Looks like something I will need to test in my future vibration tube array.

text: I would suggest to make a simple calculation of forces, friction, levers and torque (moment). Longer the lever, greater the torque. So support of the drum should be as close as possible to the center axis. Maybe it would be better to stabilise the drum by hanging it on the axis from the top. Bottom support far away from center, makes a long lever. So even a small friction crates considerable torque, which stops rotation. So you are throwing away a lot of energy because of log levers. It is much easier to shorten the levers, than make less friction.

author: @stevecummins324
text:
car alternators(aprox 1kW output ) use DC electromagnets to generate magnetic  field.
no current through the field coils... very small field(due to low residual magnetism in poles) low output current, and low starting/running torque.
lots of current through the field coils... lots of output current, but high starting torque.

yes a little of the output is lost driving the electromagnets...but it's far more flexible, and can dynamically match "input power" to electrical load, thus resulting in better off design point operation

author: @hanslepoeter5167
text:
Oh ... Cores and close gaps between magnets and stator core material are much better in terms of performance. Slowing down of the turbine only means it is delivering electrical power so that's a good thing. Not a bad thing. Even if you optimize for low wind speeds the rpm should lower at electrical load. The way you have it now is bad in terms of power delivered. Also for low wind speeds. It's excellent for voltage as you pointed out but you moved far far away from your 1kW target power at any wind speed. 1 microwave oven transformer probably does better than 50 turn table coils wo core in terms of energy. 

For demonstration purpose it is excellent. Sure.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I se what you are saying mate but it's not strictly true I think cored and coreless designs are pretty common each has it's uses depending on the situation - if you have a core then there is magnetic attraction between the core and the magnet the device has to overcome to turn and therefore generate raising the stating torque requirement - or at least that is the way I am seeing it

replies.author: @hanslepoeter5167
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Hi Robert, thanks for the answer. Magnet attraction is like cycling uphil. You need energy to do that but you get it back when you go downhill. It's lossless in the end. So you need a starting torque to get the thing running but once it does magnetic attraction does not account for losses.  Cores do cause losses because currents are caused in the core. This is why these cores are laminated. It does not allow current to flow from one plate to the other to minimize losses. Cores do contribute to magnetic coupling significantly which is way more important than the losses in the core.  So commercial generators all will have a core. Then again, if you have an energy harvester where only a few mW output is important for as much time as possible a coreless design can be exactly what you need. But whatever you do it is an excellent project for any of us to learn something new.

replies.author: @Iamsuccesspro
replies.text:
​@ThinkingandTinkering it's called cogging, that's why guys that make huge wind generators have only coils, more coils, more copper, no cogging. 
Size of coil has to correspond to size magnets, so voltages happen at the same time and they all add up. Then at the end you have a rectifier and caps etc. 
A good book to read on this stuff is by Paul Gype I think. 
Anyway thanks for sharing and learning. Gotta love it!

author: @goldmagnet9013
text: Rob, taking on board what you said in this video you may want to change the title of this video/project, its a long way from the project you just explained both in terms of output and cost. Also why not put all the capacitors down below instead of up on the mast, I may be wrong but the look like they are wired in parallel, so it seems there is no reason to have them up on the mast at all, keeps weight down and reduces weather exposure to points of failure.. Any chance of letting us know the resistance of those coils so we can calculate maximum power output ? I must admit the design with a 0.5v drop on every coil seems suicidal Vs series coils with 0.5v drop on each phase, you are throwing away 10-20% of your total available power right there (at 2-2.5v per coil).. As the magnet spacing is too close for your new coils I guess you are stuck there unless you can work out some balanced phases using the current spacing. Friction is super important for generating at low speeds, but yours seems too have lots of friction compared to other  turbines, that bearing seems better than the castors, but still pretty poor.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I agree mate - the bearing is pretty poor and I am still working on that so that will change - I take on board your other comments too - I am unlikely to change the layout on the electronics with this one as it is pretty much just working out ideas and ease of access is more important right now. The voltage recorded isn't the coil output voltage it's the charge on the capacitor bank. But I can measure resistance and coil output for you easily enough mate - cheers
